title: Current ASTM and ASME Standard Methods
narrationScript: Every penetrant test you will ever run answers to a document. Before the first drop of penetrant touches a part, somebody decided which standard governs the job, and that decision controls your materials, your dwell times, your lighting, and what counts as a rejectable indication. This lesson sorts out the family of standard practices behind liquid penetrant testing so you can read a job package and know exactly which rules you are working under. Start with the umbrella. ASTM E165 is the standard practice for liquid penetrant examination for general industry. It covers the whole method. Both penetrant types live there, Type one fluorescent and Type two visible dye. So do all four removal methods, water washable, lipophilic post emulsifiable, solvent removable, and hydrophilic post emulsifiable. If you need one document that lays out the entire penetrant toolbox, E165 is it. E165 matters beyond ASTM because the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code adopts it. When you test pressure vessels, boilers, or power piping, Section Five, Article Six is the code article that tells you how the examination must be performed, and it carries an adopted copy of E165 under the designation SE-165. Here is the distinction people miss. Article Six and E165 tell you how to test. They do not tell you whether the part passes. Acceptance criteria come from the referencing code section or the contract, the vessel code, the piping code, whatever document sent the part to you. Never pull accept and reject limits out of a practice that was never meant to contain them. Next come the process specific practices. ASTM publishes a small set of documents that each describe one fluorescent process in detail. E1209 covers the water washable fluorescent process, which is Method A. E1208 covers the lipophilic post emulsified process, Method B. E1210 covers the hydrophilic post emulsified process, Method D. A companion document, E1219, covers the solvent removable fluorescent process, Method C. Why bother with single process documents when E165 covers everything? Because a purchase order can invoke exactly the process it wants, and a shop that runs one line can build its procedure on a document that matches that line step for step. When a spec calls out E1210, you know without reading further that you are running hydrophilic remover on a fluorescent line. Then there is the aerospace workhorse, ASTM E1417. It grew out of an older military standard for penetrant inspection, and it carries the aerospace mindset with it. Two things set it apart. First, materials. Under E1417 you cannot use just any penetrant that pours. The penetrant, the emulsifier or remover, and the developer must be qualified to the aerospace materials specification AMS 2644 and listed on its qualified products list. That specification is also where the familiar classification language lives, type, method, sensitivity level, and developer form. Second, process control. E1417 builds in the discipline this course keeps returning to, things like a daily system performance check against a known defect panel, plus periodic checks of ultraviolet intensity, water content, and remover concentration. If you work engine parts, airframe hardware, or military components, expect E1417 on the paperwork. So how do these documents reach you? Through a chain. The contract or purchase order names the governing code or practice and the revision. The code or practice sets the requirements. Your employer's written procedure, reviewed and approved by a Level three, translates those requirements into the steps you actually perform. Below that sits a technique sheet or part specific card with times, materials, and coverage for the exact part in your hands. Each level must satisfy the one above it. You work to the written procedure, and the written procedure answers up the chain. Two field habits close this lesson. First, check the revision. Standards change, and the revision named in the contract is the one that governs, not the newest one on the shelf, and not the one you memorized. Second, when documents disagree, stop. Do not split the difference. Take the conflict to your Level three and get a ruling before you test. A penetrant test run to the wrong document is not a test. It is paperwork waiting to be rejected, and rework you could have prevented by reading for two minutes before you sprayed anything.
imagePrompt: Two NDT technicians stand at the head of a fluorescent penetrant processing line of stainless steel dip tanks, one pointing down the line toward a darkened inspection booth glowing violet at the far end of the shop.

sections.heading: Why a Penetrant Test Answers to Paper
sections.body:
Penetrant testing looks simple from the outside - spray, wait, wipe, develop, look. What makes it a defensible engineering activity, repeatable from one shop to the next, is that every step is governed by a published document. The vocabulary matters:

- A **standard practice** describes *how* to perform the examination - materials, process steps, controls. The ASTM penetrant practices are this kind of document.
- A **code** (such as a pressure-vessel or piping code) *invokes* a practice, gives it contractual force, and supplies the acceptance criteria that say pass or fail.
- Your **written procedure** is your employer's translation of those requirements into shop-floor steps, reviewed and approved by an NDT Level III. It is the document you physically work to.

This lesson maps the five documents named in the CP-105 outline - ASTM E165, E1208, E1209, E1210, and E1417 - plus the ASME connection, so that when a job package lands on your bench you know which rulebook you are inside before the first can is shaken.

sections.heading: ASTM E165 and the ASME Connection
sections.body:
**ASTM E165/E165M** is the standard practice for liquid penetrant examination for general industry, and it is the umbrella over the whole method. Both penetrant types (Type I fluorescent, Type II visible) and all four removal methods (A water-washable, B lipophilic post-emulsifiable, C solvent-removable, D hydrophilic post-emulsifiable) are covered, along with materials, processing steps, and technique requirements.

E165 also leads a second life inside the boiler and pressure vessel world. **ASME Section V, Article 6** is the code article governing liquid penetrant examination for code work, and it adopts E165 - the adopted copy carries the **SE-165** designation. When a vessel or piping job says "PT per ASME Section V, Article 6," the methodology you follow is built on that adopted practice.

Keep one boundary line bright: **practices and Article 6 tell you how to examine - they do not tell you whether the part passes.** Acceptance criteria come from the *referencing* code section or the contract: the vessel code, the piping code, the purchase order's quality clauses. Pulling accept/reject limits out of a how-to practice is a classic paperwork nonconformance. Practices such as ASTM E165 and ASME Section V, Article 6 specify dwell times, lighting, and process steps - the referencing document owns the verdict.

sections.caption: The PT standards family: the general-industry track through ASTM E165 and ASME Section V Article 6, the aerospace track through ASTM E1417 with AMS 2644 qualified materials, and the process-specific fluorescent practices beneath.

sections.heading: The Process-Specific Practices: E1208, E1209, E1210
sections.body:
ASTM also publishes practices that each cover exactly one fluorescent process, in step-by-step depth:

- **E1209** - fluorescent penetrant testing using the **water-washable** process (Type I, Method A)
- **E1208** - fluorescent penetrant testing using the **lipophilic post-emulsification** process (Type I, Method B)
- **E1210** - fluorescent penetrant testing using the **hydrophilic post-emulsification** process (Type I, Method D)
- A companion practice, **E1219**, does the same for the solvent-removable fluorescent process (Type I, Method C)

Why duplicate what E165 already covers? Precision of invocation. A purchase order that cites E1210 has told you, in one document number, that you are running a hydrophilic post-emulsified fluorescent line - no ambiguity about which annex applies. A shop that runs a single process can also build its written procedure directly on the practice that matches its line, step for step.

For a Level II the practical skill is recognition: see **E1209** on a router and think *Method A fluorescent*; see **E1208**, think *lipophilic Method B*; see **E1210**, think *hydrophilic Method D*. The cross-reference table below is the habit to internalize.

sections.caption: Standard-to-method cross-reference - the recognition table worth memorizing.
rows: General-industry practice for the whole PT method
Aerospace practice - QPL materials, mandatory process controls
Code methodology - adopts E165 as SE-165; acceptance per referencing code

sections.heading: ASTM E1417: The Aerospace Practice
sections.body:
**ASTM E1417/E1417M** is the penetrant practice of the aerospace and defense world. It descends from a superseded military standard for penetrant inspection (MIL-STD-6866), and it imported the military's process-control mindset. Two features set it apart from general-industry work.

**Qualified materials.** Under E1417 you cannot substitute any penetrant that happens to pour. Penetrant materials - penetrants, emulsifiers, removers, developers - must be qualified to **SAE AMS 2644** and listed on its qualified products list (QPL). AMS 2644 is also where the familiar classification system lives: Type, Method, fluorescent **sensitivity levels ½ through 4**, and developer forms. When your procedure says "Type I, Method D, Level 3," that language traces to the materials specification.

**Mandatory process control.** E1417 requires the discipline this course keeps returning to: a daily system performance check against a known-defect comparator panel, plus periodic checks of UV-A intensity, penetrant contamination and water content, remover concentration, developer condition, and bath temperatures - each on a defined interval.

Expect E1417 on engine hardware, airframe structure, and military components. Expect, too, that an auditor working to it will ask for your control-check records before asking about your indications - under that practice, an examination without the process checks behind it is no examination at all.

sections.title: The practice says how - the contract says pass or fail
sections.body: Never take acceptance criteria from a standard practice. The chain runs: **contract or PO** names the code/practice and revision → **code or practice** sets the methodology → **your written procedure** (Level III approved) turns it into steps → **technique or part card** gives part-specific details. Each level must satisfy the one above it. If two documents disagree, stop and get a Level III ruling - do not improvise a compromise.

sections.caption: Order of precedence: requirements flow down from the contract through the governing practice to the written procedure and technique card you actually work to.

sections.heading: Read the Invocation Before You Spray
sections.body:
A Level II's two-minute paperwork check, before any penetrant goes on:

- **Document and revision.** The contract's named revision governs - not the newest one on the shelf. Confirm the procedure in your hand was written to it.
- **Type, method, sensitivity level.** Match what the router or PO calls out against the materials actually staged at your station. On E1417 work, verify the materials are the QPL-listed ones your procedure names - and from the same family, since intermixing materials from different manufacturers' systems is not permitted without qualification.
- **Acceptance criteria source.** Know which document owns pass/fail before you find an indication, not after.
- **Process-control status.** Daily system performance check done and logged? Lighting verified? If not, the line is not ready, whatever the schedule says.

Thirty seconds per item. That habit is the difference between an examination and an expensive guess - and it is exactly what an auditor reconstructs when a test is challenged months later.

figures.title: Order of precedence from contract to examination

checkQuestions.question: A job traveler calls for liquid penetrant examination per the general-industry ASTM practice that covers both types and all four removal methods. Which document is it pointing to?
options: ASTM E1208
ASTM E165/E165M
ASTM E1417/E1417M
ASTM E1210
checkQuestions.explanation: E165/E165M is the general-industry umbrella practice covering Type I and Type II and Methods A through D; E1208 and E1210 are single-process documents and E1417 is the aerospace practice.

checkQuestions.question: You are performing PT on a pressure vessel "per ASME Section V, Article 6." Where do the accept/reject criteria for indications come from?
options: Article 6 itself - it is the governing methodology
The penetrant manufacturer's technical data sheet
The referencing code section or contract that invoked Article 6
The Level II's judgment based on indication brightness
checkQuestions.explanation: Article 6 (built on the adopted SE-165 practice) tells you how to examine; acceptance criteria belong to the referencing code section or contract that sent the part to you.

checkQuestions.question: Which ASTM practice specifically governs the water-washable fluorescent penetrant process?
options: ASTM E1209
ASTM E1208
ASTM E1210
ASTM E1219
checkQuestions.explanation: E1209 covers the water-washable fluorescent process (Method A); E1208 covers lipophilic Method B, E1210 covers hydrophilic Method D, and E1219 covers solvent-removable Method C.

checkQuestions.question: A purchase order invokes ASTM E1417 for engine hardware. What does that require of your penetrant materials?
options: Any materials in the company storeroom may be used if they are fresh
Materials must be visible dye only
Materials may be mixed across manufacturers' families to optimize sensitivity
Materials must be qualified to AMS 2644 and listed on its qualified products list
checkQuestions.explanation: E1417 requires penetrant materials qualified to AMS 2644 and listed on the QPL, used as a compatible family - substitution or intermixing without qualification is not permitted.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement best captures the practical difference between E165 and E1417?
options: E165 covers fluorescent penetrants only, while E1417 covers visible dye only
E1417 adds aerospace-grade requirements: QPL-listed materials and mandatory periodic process-control checks
E165 contains acceptance criteria, while E1417 does not
E1417 applies only to Method C solvent-removable work
checkQuestions.explanation: Both cover the full range of types and methods; E1417's distinction is its aerospace heritage - qualified materials per AMS 2644 and a mandatory schedule of process-control checks such as the daily system performance test.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §3.1 (current ASTM and ASME standard methods)
ASTM E165/E165M, general-industry liquid penetrant practice (cited generically)
ASTM E1208, E1209, E1210, E1219, process-specific fluorescent practices (cited generically)
ASTM E1417/E1417M with SAE AMS 2644 QPL, aerospace practice and materials qualification (cited generically)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6 - code methodology; acceptance criteria per the referencing code
